student-and-enrollment-model.label: Student and enrollment model
student-and-enrollment-model.summary: Explore how to design a student and enrollment model in relational databases, focusing on separating entities and understanding many-to-many relationships.

ci1.title: Create a linking table for enrollments

fb1.title: Identify the linking table
fb1.prompt: In a student enrollment system, which table connects students and courses in a many-to-many relationship?
fb1.hint: It's not the table with student or course details.

help.concept: A linking table is used to represent many-to-many relationships by storing pairs of related IDs.
help.hint_1: The linking table contains references to both students and courses.
help.hint_2: This table tracks which students are enrolled in which courses.

fb1.template: The table that connects students and courses is called the ___.

fb2.title: Purpose of separating tables
fb2.prompt: Why do we keep student details and enrollment records in separate tables?
fb2.hint: Think about what happens if you repeat student info for every enrollment.

help.concept: Separating tables prevents repeating the same information multiple times, which can lead to errors.
help.hint_1: Keeping data in one place makes updates easier and more accurate.
help.hint_2: Repeating the same student info for each enrollment is inefficient and risky.

fb2.template: We separate student details and enrollments to avoid ___.

sc1.title: Relationship type
sc1.prompt: What type of relationship exists between students and courses in an enrollment system?
sc1.hint: Focus on the main concept being tested and eliminate choices that describe something different.

help.concept: Choose the option that matches the core idea described in the question without relying on repeated wording from the answer choices.
help.hint_1: Rule out options that describe a different role, behavior, or SQL concept than the question is testing.
help.hint_2: Pick the option that best matches the concept, not the one that simply repeats familiar wording.

mc1.title: Benefits of using a linking table
mc1.prompt: Which of the following are benefits of using a linking (enrollments) table in a student-course model? Select all that apply.
mc1.hint: Think about maintenance, accuracy, and flexibility.

help.concept: A linking table helps keep data organized and avoids repeating information.
help.hint_1: It helps you update student or course details in one place.
help.hint_2: It does not require every student to enroll in every course.

options.b: Makes it easier to update student or course info
options.c: Allows tracking of which students are in which courses
options.d: Forces all students to enroll in every course

ci1.title: Create a linking table for enrollments
ci1.prompt: Write a SQL statement to create an enrollments table that links students and courses by their IDs. Assume both students and courses tables already exist with primary keys student_id and course_id.
ci1.hint: Focus on the SQL task being asked for, not on copying final query text.

help.concept: Build the query from the operation the exercise is testing.
help.hint_1: Think about which clauses or functions are required for the task.
help.hint_2: Construct the query based on what result the exercise expects, not by repeating exact solution wording.

ci1.starterCode: -- Write a CREATE TABLE statement for enrollments

sk1.title: Why separate students and enrollments?
sk1.bodyMarkdown: In relational database design, it's important to avoid repeating information. For example, if you store every student's course enrollments in the same table as their personal details, you risk duplicating student data for each enrollment. Instead, you should create a separate **students** table for student details and an **enrollments** table to track which students are enrolled in which courses. This approach reduces duplication and makes updates easier.

sk2.title: Modeling many-to-many relationships
sk2.bodyMarkdown: A student can enroll in many courses, and each course can have many students. This is a classic **many-to-many** relationship. To represent this in SQL, you use a third table (often called a join or linking table), such as **enrollments**, which contains references (foreign keys) to both the **students** and **courses** tables. This way, each enrollment links one student to one course, and you can track all enrollments without duplicating data.
